required.title: [Read Aloud] D'Aulaires' Book of Greek Myths
required.author: Ingri and Edgar Parin d'Aulaire

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] The Wind in the Willows
suggested.author: Kenneth Grahame

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Grimm's Fairy Tales
suggested.author: — a complete or near-complete edition, not a sanitized retelling

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Norse Myths
suggested.author: retold by Kevin Crossley-Holland or *D'Aulaires' Book of Norse Myths*

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Selected Bible stories — the King James Version or a faithful retelling
[Read Aloud] A Child's Garden of Verses
suggested.author: Robert Louis Stevenson

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Selected poems by Robert Frost and Emily Dickinson
suggested.author: Robert Frost and Emily Dickinson

supplementary.title: [For Mentor] Honey for a Child's Heart
supplementary.author: Gladys Hunt

competence_markers.description: The student has been read to daily from works of genuine literary quality for the duration of the packet
The student can narrate a fairy tale, myth, or story chapter with accurate sequence and specific detail
The student can identify the hero of a story and explain what makes them heroic — through what they did, not just what they were called
The student can identify what a character wanted, what stood in the way, and what happened as a result
The student has memorized and can recite at least three poems or prose passages
The student demonstrates the habit of thinking about stories — asking "why" questions, noticing character choices, expressing opinions with reasons
The student can express a preference among stories read and articulate why — moving beyond "I liked it" to "I liked it because..."
The student shows growing attention span and engagement during read-aloud sessions over the course of the packet

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story of [specific fairy tale just read].
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? Who was the hero? What made them the hero? What happened to the characters who made bad choices?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about [specific Greek god or myth].
narration_prompts.detail: Who were they? What did they do? What happened because of what they did?
Then tell me: what do you think the poem is about? What is your favorite line? Why?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the book we just finished reading together.
narration_prompts.detail: Not just what happened — what was it about? What do you think the author wanted you to think about?

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick two stories we read and tell me how they are alike and how they are different.
narration_prompts.detail: What did both stories care about? What was different about how they told their stories?

narration_prompts.prompt: Was there a sentence or a passage from any of our readings that you remember especially well?
narration_prompts.detail: What was it? Why do you think it stayed with you?

cross_connections.description: Narration after reading builds the same skills developed in 3.2.1. The student who retells a story orally is practicing organized expression; the student who writes narrations is practicing the craft of putting thoughts on paper. Literature and writing reinforce each other at every stage.

While 3.2.4-A focuses on the experience of great literature — hearing it, absorbing it, memorizing it — 3.2.5-A develops the habit of asking what stories teach and what they want you to believe. The two sub-domains work in parallel: 3.2.4 builds literary experience; 3.2.5 builds literary awareness.

The student who has been immersed in great stories naturally begins to tell and write their own. The fairy tales, myths, and novels encountered here are the models — often unconscious — for the student's own creative work in 3.2.3.

The myths and sacred stories encountered here — Greek, Norse, biblical — are the same stories studied in 1.3.1 as expressions of worldview. Literature introduces them as stories; 1.3.1 explores what they reveal about what people believe.

The myths and epics encountered in 3.2.4-A are products of the civilizations studied in 1.1.1. The student who reads Greek myths while studying ancient Greece sees the stories as part of a living civilization, not as disconnected entertainment.

The vocabulary, sentence structure, and language patterns absorbed through daily read-alouds reinforce the grammatical awareness being built in 3.1.1. Immersion in great prose is one of the most powerful ways to internalize the structures of language.

content.why_this_matters:
You are going to meet some of the greatest stories human beings have ever told. These stories are very old — some of them are thousands of years old — and people are still telling them, still reading them, still arguing about what they mean. That should tell you something: these are not ordinary stories. They are the ones that lasted, because every generation of readers found something in them that mattered.

Right now, the most important thing is not that you understand every word or every idea. The most important thing is that you hear these stories, that you live inside them while they are being read to you, and that you start to notice things. What did the character do? Why did they do it? What happened because of their choice? What would you have done? These are the questions that turn you from someone who hears stories into someone who thinks about stories. And a person who thinks about stories is a person who thinks about life — because stories are how human beings have always made sense of what it means to be alive.

You will hear fairy tales that are darker and stranger than the versions you may already know. You will hear myths about gods and heroes who do terrible and wonderful things. You will hear passages from books written by people who chose every single word with care, and you will start to feel the difference between writing that is merely fine and writing that is alive. You do not need to analyze this difference yet. You need to experience it. The analysis will come later, when you are ready. For now, the goal is immersion: great stories, read aloud, every day, with someone who cares about them.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **A habit of daily encounter with great literature** — the child hears stories of genuine literary quality every day, building an appetite for real books
2. **Listening comprehension and attention span** — the ability to follow a sustained narrative read aloud, tracking characters, events, and meaning
3. **Narrative awareness** — the child begins to notice story structure: character, problem, resolution, consequence
4. **Vocabulary through immersion** — the rhythms and words of great prose and poetry enter the child's language naturally through repeated exposure
5. **The habit of thinking about stories** — asking why, not just what; noticing choices, not just events
6. **Memorization and recitation** — the child internalizes great language by committing poems and passages to memory
7. **The foundation for a lifelong reading life** — the child who is read to with love and engagement becomes the adult who reads by choice
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Is a Story, and Why Does It Matter?

Human beings have been telling stories for as long as there have been human beings. Before anyone could write, people sat around fires and told stories — about where they came from, about what the gods did, about heroes who fought monsters and clever animals who outsmarted bigger animals. Cave paintings from 30,000 years ago may be the earliest "illustrations" of stories.

Why do people tell stories? Because stories are how we understand the world. When something happens to you — something confusing or frightening or wonderful — you turn it into a story. You tell someone: "Let me tell you what happened." And in the telling, you start to make sense of it. Stories put events in order. They show causes and effects. They let you see what happens when someone makes a choice.

The stories you are going to read in this packet are not just any stories. They are the ones that human beings have kept alive for hundreds or thousands of years. Out of all the millions of stories ever told, these are the ones people could not let go of. That means there is something in them that matters — something that every generation recognizes as true about what it means to be human.

### Part 2: Fairy Tales — Stories That Teach Without Lecturing

Fairy tales are some of the oldest stories we have. The ones collected by the Brothers Grimm in Germany, by Charles Perrault in France, and by storytellers across every culture share remarkable patterns: a hero who is young, small, or overlooked; a challenge that seems impossible; a world where courage and kindness are rewarded and cruelty is punished — though not always right away, and not always in the way you expect.

The original fairy tales are not gentle. In the Grimm version of Cinderella, the stepsisters cut off parts of their feet to fit into the slipper. In the original Little Red Riding Hood, the wolf eats the grandmother and there is no rescue. These details were not accidents — they were warnings. Fairy tales taught children that the world contains danger, that people are not always what they seem, and that your choices matter. They did not protect children from hard truths. They prepared children for them.

When you read fairy tales, notice: Who is the hero? What makes them the hero — is it strength, or something else? What happens to the characters who are greedy, dishonest, or cruel? What does the story think is most important?

### Part 3: Myths — Stories About the Biggest Questions

Myths are different from fairy tales. Fairy tales happen in a vague "once upon a time." Myths happen in a specific world with specific gods, specific rules, and specific consequences. The Greek myths tell you about Zeus, Athena, Poseidon, and a world where the gods are powerful but not good — where they are jealous, vain, and dangerous. The Norse myths tell you about Odin, Thor, and Loki in a world that is heading toward destruction — Ragnarok, the end of everything — and the gods know it and fight anyway.

Myths answer the biggest questions people can ask: Where did the world come from? Why do bad things happen? What makes a hero? What happens after death? Different cultures gave different answers to these questions, and those answers shaped everything those cultures built, believed, and valued.

When you hear myths, you are hearing the oldest answers human beings gave to the hardest questions. You do not have to agree with the answers. But you should listen to them carefully, because they are still shaping the world you live in — even if you do not know it yet.

### Part 4: Great Books — Writing That Is Alive

Some books are written so well that the words seem to come alive when you hear them. When E.B. White writes the opening of *Charlotte's Web* — "Where's Papa going with that ax?" — you are immediately there, in the kitchen, wondering the same thing Fern is wondering. When C.S. Lewis describes Aslan, you feel the combination of fear and longing that the children in the story feel. When Kipling tells you how the elephant got its trunk, the rhythm of the sentences makes you want to read them again.

This is not an accident. These writers chose every word. They spent hours deciding whether to use this word or that word, this sentence rhythm or that one. The reason their books have lasted for decades or centuries is that the writing itself — not just the story, but the way the story is told — is extraordinary.

You do not need to understand how they did it yet. That will come later. Right now, your job is to notice when language is doing something special. When a sentence makes you laugh, or makes the hair on the back of your neck stand up, or makes you see something so clearly that you feel like you are there — notice that. Remember it. That is the beginning of close reading: paying attention to what the words are doing, not just what they are saying.

### Part 5: Memorization — Making Great Words Your Own

For thousands of years, children memorized poetry and great passages of prose. They recited them aloud. They carried those words in their memory for the rest of their lives. This practice has largely been abandoned in modern schools, and something real was lost.

When you memorize a poem, the words become yours. They live in your mind. You can call them up when you need them — when you see something beautiful and Robert Frost's words come to you, when you are afraid and the words of the Psalm come to you, when you are trying to say something and the right words are already there because you memorized them years ago.

Memorization also teaches you something about language that nothing else can teach in the same way. When you memorize a sentence or a poem, you feel its rhythm in your body. You feel where the stresses fall, where the pauses are, how the sounds work together. This physical knowledge of language rhythm is the foundation of all good writing and all good public speaking. It cannot be taught from a textbook. It can only be learned by taking great words into your memory and speaking them aloud.

### Part 6: The Habit of Paying Attention

The most important thing this packet develops is not knowledge about specific stories. It is a habit: the habit of paying attention when a story is being told. Paying attention means more than just sitting still and being quiet. It means listening with your mind engaged — following the characters, noticing what they do, wondering why they do it, caring about what happens to them.

This habit of attention is the foundation of everything you will do later with literature. In Weathering Storms, you will read whole books on your own and learn to analyze what the author is doing. In Standing Ground, you will read the greatest works ever written and write essays about them. None of that is possible without the foundation built here: a person who pays attention to stories, who thinks about what they hear, and who has developed a taste for writing that is genuinely good.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Daily Read-Aloud [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Read aloud to the student every day from the required and suggested reading lists. A minimum of 20 minutes per session; 30-45 minutes is better. After each reading session, the student narrates what they heard — in their own words, with as much detail as they can produce. The narration is the primary assessment.

### Study 2: Fairy Tale Collection [Early in the Packet]
Read a selection of Grimm's fairy tales (at least 10, including both well-known and lesser-known stories). For each, the student narrates the story back and discusses: Who was the hero? What made them heroic? What happened to characters who made bad choices? Keep a simple list of fairy tales read, with the student's one-sentence summary of each.

### Study 3: Greek Myths Immersion [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Work through *D'Aulaires' Book of Greek Myths* over several weeks. After each reading, the student narrates. Key discussions: What were the Greek gods like? Were they good? What happened to humans who made the gods angry? What does this tell you about what the Greeks believed about the world? Create a simple "family tree" of the gods together.

### Study 4: Memorization and Recitation [Ongoing Throughout Packet]
The student memorizes at least three poems or prose passages during this packet. Suggested starting points: "The Tyger" by William Blake, Psalm 23, "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening" by Robert Frost, or a passage from *The Hobbit* or *Narnia*. The student recites each memorized piece to a family member or small group.

### Study 5: One Great Novel [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Read *Charlotte's Web* or *The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe* (or both, if time allows) as a sustained read-aloud over several weeks. After finishing, discuss: What was the story really about — not just what happened, but what it was about? What did the characters learn? What do you think the author wanted you to think about?

### Study 6: Illustration as Response [Ongoing]
After key readings, the student draws a scene from the story. This is not busywork — to draw a scene accurately, the student must pay attention to what the text actually described. Compare the student's illustration to the text: did you include the details the author mentioned? What did you add from your own imagination?

### Study 7: "What Did You Notice?" Conversations [End of Packet]
As the packet nears completion, hold conversations that look back across all the stories read: Which story was your favorite? Why? Which character do you think about the most? Was there a story that surprised you or that you did not like? What made the writing in some books feel different from others? These conversations are informal but important — they develop the reflective habit that close reading requires.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Narration Quality
**Developing well:** Narrations are detailed, sequenced, and include personal interpretation. The student remembers character names, key events, and can explain why things happened, not just what happened. Narrations grow longer and more organized over weeks.

**Developing slowly:** Narrations are single-sentence summaries or confused sequences. The student cannot recall specific details even shortly after reading.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the reading material at the right level for this child's listening comprehension? Are sessions at a time when the child is alert? Are you reading with engagement? Is the narration prompt specific enough?

### 2. Memorization and Recitation
**Developing well:** The student can recite memorized passages with confidence and expression. They enjoy the process and may begin memorizing things on their own.

**Developing slowly:** The student struggles to retain even short passages after repeated practice.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the selected passages short enough to start with? (Begin with four to six lines and build up.) Is the child practicing daily in short sessions rather than cramming? Is the material engaging to them specifically?

### 3. Thinking About Stories
**Developing well:** The student spontaneously asks "why" questions about characters and events. They offer opinions with reasons. They connect stories to their own experience: "That's like when I..." They notice when stories remind them of other stories.

**Developing slowly:** The student consumes stories passively — enjoys listening but does not engage with the material beyond the surface. No questions emerge. Opinions are limited to "I liked it" or "it was boring."

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling curiosity? After reading, do you share your own reactions — "I always wondered why he did that" or "That part surprises me every time"? The child who sees an adult thinking about stories learns that thinking about stories is what adults do.

### 4. Growing Attention Span
**Developing well:** The student can sustain attention through a 30-45 minute read-aloud by the end of the packet. They resist interruptions and ask for "one more chapter."

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot sustain attention beyond 10-15 minutes even at the end of the packet. Fidgeting, distraction, and disengagement are persistent.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the reading environment free of distractions? Is the material at the right level — too easy is boring, too hard is frustrating? Is the child getting enough physical activity before reading sessions? Has the child been screened for attention-related concerns if the problem persists despite good conditions?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Reading Below the Child's Level
Children's literature is not all the same quality. Books written to a reading level rather than to a literary standard are often dull — and children know it. A child who is read *Charlotte's Web* or *The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe* is hearing prose of genuine literary quality. A child who is read only leveled readers and commercial children's books is not. The read-aloud does not need to be limited to books the child could read independently — it should be at or above their listening comprehension level. Read up, not down.

### Skipping Narration
It is tempting to skip narration when time is short or when the child "obviously got it." Do not skip it. Narration is not just assessment — it is the cognitive act that transforms passive listening into active learning. The child who narrates retains far more than the child who merely listens. This is one of the best-supported findings in educational research.

### Explaining Too Much
When the child encounters an unfamiliar word or concept, the temptation is to stop and explain. Resist this except when the unfamiliar element will prevent the child from following the story. Most of the time, context makes the meaning clear enough. Constant interruption for vocabulary instruction destroys the reading experience. If the child asks, answer briefly and return to the story. If they do not ask, they may have understood more from context than you think.

### Making Literature a Chore
If the child begins to dread "reading time," something is wrong. The fix is usually environmental, not curricular: read at a time when the child is alert and comfortable. Read on the couch, on the porch, in a blanket fort. Let the child hold the book and look at illustrations. Bring a snack. The association between reading and pleasure is the single most important outcome of Stretching Branches literature education.

### Neglecting Poetry
Poetry is easy to skip because it does not have the narrative pull of a story. Do not skip it. Memorizing and reciting poetry builds a relationship with language that prose alone cannot provide. The child who carries poems in their memory has resources of language that will serve them for life.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Fairy Tale Narration
**Strong:** Retells the story with accurate sequence, includes key details, identifies the hero and explains what made them heroic, notes consequences for characters who made poor choices. May add personal commentary: "I think the wolf deserved what happened because..."
**Weak:** "There was a wolf and he was bad." No sequence, no detail, no engagement.

### Prompt 2: Greek Myth Narration
**Strong:** Names specific gods or heroes, retells the myth with sequence and detail, can explain what the characters wanted and what happened because of their actions. May express an opinion about whether the gods were fair.
**Weak:** "It was about Greek gods." Cannot name specific characters or events.

### Prompt 3: Poetry Recitation
**Strong:** Recites with confidence and some expression. Can say what they think the poem is about, even if the interpretation is simple. Has a favorite line and can say why.
**Weak:** Cannot remember the poem even after practice. No connection to the meaning of the words.

### Prompt 4: Novel Discussion
**Strong:** Can discuss the book beyond plot summary — identifies what the book was "really about," connects it to their own experience, has opinions with reasons, asks questions the book raised for them.
**Weak:** Can only summarize events. No interpretation, no personal connection, no questions.
